title: The Self-Hosting Barrier to Entry
summary: I meet a lot of nerds—some are even of the Computer Variety! Since I'm obsessed with RSS , my first 1 question boils down to "do you have an RSS feed I can subscribe to?" You know what they say about assuming. Time Building a website is time-consuming! Doing anything more than slapping together something in HTML and throwing it on GitHub pages in thirty min…
content:
I meet a lot of nerds—some are even of the Computer Variety! Since I'm obsessed with RSS, my first1 question boils down to "do you have an RSS feed I can subscribe to?"

You know what they say about assuming.

Time

Building a website is time-consuming! Doing anything more than slapping together something in HTML and throwing it on GitHub pages in thirty minutes is going to take more than said thirty minutes.

I think that I have spent more hours than anybody really ought to on making my website. There are always problems. There are always opportunities to redesign and change the theme. There are always opportunities to extend the website to do LaTeX.

There is always something more to do. Something else to fix.

  • A bug in the RSS feed generator causes the feeds to not update sometimes.
  • Sometimes SSL just refuses to work, until you disable and reenable SSL, at which point it'll work for a few more days before breaking again.
  • The files being served only load partway before stopping
  • /wp-admin is very popular, for some reason
  • Dear god how is there much spam from HTML forms

Maintaining the security of the website is rewarding, but it honestly gets exhausting after a while. Considering that my nerd friends are all students at university, we don't have the time between studying to work on tweaking and maintaining a website, much less keep it up-to-date with new content.

A website is a commitment, and many people have their priorities in (the right) order: personal projects below The Important Bits.

Money

Running a website isn't exactly cheap either. Unless if you were to go for a free webhost or use a .github.io (or whatever) subdomain, or sign up for Neocities (100% recommend) running a website has a significant cost. Hardware isn't expensive, but it isn't cheap either.

If you want control over your website, i.e. start self-hosting beyond a static website, you'll need some kind of VPS or a piece of hardware running in your house. Many companies do offer free VPSs, and Oracle is especially generous with their free-tier Ampere devices, although these programs tend to be flaky and restrictive in what you can and cannot do, have long waitlists for getting access, and will terminate your VM if you aren't "using it enough" (happened to my friend's Oracle free-tier VPS). Hopefully you paid for backups.

Philosophy

Finally, the biggest resistance I've seen to not setting up a website is "I don't care".

To be honest, that's perfectly fine. You need to be willing to make a website in order to burn dozens of hours on it.

To some people, the reasoning for not making a website are self-evident. Others think the opposite. Why start a website? Why spend precious free-time on it? Why should you be passionate?

I could rehash the whole "take back the indie web" schtick that many​ folks​ have​ written about already, but I feel like there's a more compelling argument: The Challenge.

Building and maintaining a server is a challenge. It's a problem you are meant to overcome. It's a new experience. And why do we tinker with computers if not for the challenge? Participating in the indie web is a challenge. Building the server in a limited time on a shoestring budget is also a challenge.

The challenge is what makes it enjoyable. The challenge is what I love.

I ought not to push people to make a website because of some strange misplaced sense of "prestige". I ought to push them because I know they'll enjoy the challenge.

And perhaps you will enjoy that challenge too.

Maybe I'll write something eventually about how to start with self-hosting, from end-to-end. It's a bit of a big project time-wise and research-wise, but maybe I can put it out in a month or so (no promises).

Remember when I promised to put out an article every week? Pepperidge farm remembers.

Corrections? Comments? Just wanna say hi? Get in touch!

Be sure to check out the notes! I've been trying to update that fairly regularly.

I'm also back on Mastodon. Don't make me regret this.

  1. After asking if they've tried Nix, of course ↩
